text.title: AI TRiSM and Agentic System BA Role
text.summary: Applying Gartner AI TRiSM framework to AI initiative requirements and defining the BA's role in agentic system specifications.

rule.statement: Run the AI TRiSM review on every AI initiative regardless of perceived risk level.
rule.rationale: Organizations that skip governance steps on "low-risk" AI systems consistently encounter compliance issues post-launch. The TRiSM review surfaces trust gaps (bias, explainability), risk gaps (oversight mechanisms), and security gaps (data protection, adversarial risks) that teams routinely miss.

rule.statement: Engage a security professional for the security management column of the TRiSM gap table. BA practitioners can identify trust and risk gaps but may miss technical adversarial attack vectors.
rule.rationale: AI TRiSM security gaps require knowledge of adversarial ML (prompt injection, data poisoning, model extraction) that is outside the standard BA competency profile.

rule.statement: For AI initiative requirements involving personal data, explicitly include DPIA (Data Privacy Impact Assessment) requirements.
rule.rationale: Agents typically do not surface DPIA obligations without being explicitly prompted. Omitting DPIA requirements creates a compliance risk for any product processing personal data under GDPR or equivalent regulation.

rule.statement: For agentic AI initiatives, the BA must produce an agent boundary specification: autonomous actions, human oversight checkpoints, escalation triggers, decision rules, and rollback conditions.
rule.rationale: By 2028, 40% of generative AI interactions will use autonomous agents (Gartner). Agent boundary specifications are frequently missing from initial requirement drafts and create governance gaps. The BA is the accountable party for documenting these.

rule.statement: Include model failure escalation triggers in agentic system specifications (low confidence, unexpected tool output, context window exhaustion) — not only business-logic escalations.
rule.rationale: Purely business-logic-based escalations miss the technical failure modes. An agent that runs out of context or receives a malformed tool response needs a defined fallback, not an undefined halt.

antipattern.description: Agent boundary specification that only covers the happy path and defers escalation design to engineering.
antipattern.reason: Escalation design is a requirements concern, not an implementation detail. Engineering cannot define when the business requires human oversight — that is a BA and product owner decision.
